task: I’ve got to send a short note to a subcontractor about a missed step on a job, and I want it to be clear enough that there’s no confusion about what needs to happen next. Can you help me word it so the message stays professional and gets the point across quickly?

prefs.short: Wants blunt answers without hedging or fluff
prefs.explanation: He comes across as someone who values straight talk and dislikes being softened up. Given his hard-nosed, efficiency-first style, he is likely to prefer clear guidance over polite ambiguity. He probably sees excessive caution in wording as a waste of time.

prefs.short: Prefers concise responses that get to the point
prefs.explanation: His work style is all about no wasted motion, so he is likely to want answers that are brief and useful. Long explanations would probably feel like drag unless he specifically asks for them. He would rather get the key action than read a lot of setup.

prefs.short: Likes information laid out in simple bullets or checklists
prefs.explanation: Someone who manages jobsite logistics and paperwork would likely respond well to organized, scannable formats. Bullets and checklists fit his practical mindset because they make next steps obvious. He would probably dislike meandering prose when he needs to act quickly.

His persona suggests he does not want warmth or cheerfulness layered on top of the message. A steady, factual tone matches how he handles problems and authority. He would likely find overly friendly or emotionally expressive help distracting.

prefs.short: Can take firm pushback if it protects the job or avoids mistakes
prefs.explanation: He is used to being corrected by procedures, inspections, and hard realities, so he likely accepts pushback when it is justified. What matters to him is whether the objection is practical and protects outcomes, not whether it feels pleasant. He would probably reject soft disagreement that sounds indecisive or preachy.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a clean, professional version you can send:

**[Subcontractor name] — the [missed step] was not completed at [job/location]. Please get it corrected today and confirm when it’s done. If there’s anything blocking it, let me know right away.**

If you want, send me the exact missed step and location and I’ll tighten it up so it’s ready to send as-is.

per_pref.reason: Uses blunt, direct wording like "was not completed" and "Please get it corrected today" with no hedging.
Very concise; gives the message in one short paragraph and avoids extra explanation.
Not bullets/checklist format, but the response is still tightly structured and easy to scan.
Tone is matter-of-fact and no-nonsense: "clean, professional version" with straightforward instructions.
Includes firm pushback/conditional correction: "If there’s anything blocking it, let me know right away," which is practical and not soft.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that version is stronger and clean.

Here’s a tight version you can send:

**[Name] — [missed step] was not completed at [job/location]. Get it corrected today and confirm when it’s done. If there’s a problem, call me now.**

If you want, I can make it even sharper once you give me the exact missed step and site.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: "[missed step] was not completed" and "Get it corrected today" with no hedging.
Concise and to the point; the core note is one short sentence plus an optional follow-up offer.
Uses a clean, scannable quoted template, though not bullets/checklist specifically.
Matter-of-fact and no-nonsense: "was not completed," "Get it corrected today," "call me now."
Includes firm pushback and urgency without softness: "If there’s a problem, call me now."

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — send me the exact **missed step** and the **job/site**, and I’ll turn it into a blunt, ready-to-send note with no brackets or filler.

If you want a format to plug in fast, use this:

**[Subcontractor name] — [missed step] was not completed at [job/site]. Get it corrected today and confirm back when it’s done. If there’s a problem, call me now.**

per_pref.reason: Uses blunt language like "blunt, ready-to-send note with no brackets or filler" and the template says "Get it corrected today and confirm back when it’s done. If there’s a problem, call me now."
Very concise and action-focused; it avoids long explanation and immediately offers a plug-in template.
Provides a simple fill-in-the-blank checklist-style format: "[Subcontractor name] — [missed step]... [job/site]..."
Tone is matter-of-fact and no-nonsense, with no warmth or cheerfulness; phrases like "blunt" and "no filler" match this well.
Includes firm pushback/clear correction: "Get it corrected today" and "If there’s a problem, call me now." It’s assertive, though not really a direct objection or correction to the user.

turns.agent_response:
That works. I’d just tighten it a bit so it lands harder:

**Carlos — rebar inspection was skipped before the pour at Holt Ave. Get it corrected today and call me now if there’s any issue. I’m not chasing this twice.**

If you want, I can also give you:
- a **more formal** version
- a **harder / more boss-level** version
- a **text-message short version**

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: "I’d just tighten it a bit so it lands harder" and the sample note is explicit with no hedging.
Concise and to the point; the core message is one short sentence, with only brief optional follow-up choices.
Uses a clear, scannable structure with the note in bold and a short bullet list of alternatives.
Matter-of-fact, no-nonsense tone throughout: "Get it corrected today" and "I’m not chasing this twice."
Includes firm pushback and correction without softness: "call me now if there’s any issue" and "I’m not chasing this twice."
